steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence is: “space shuttle flight STS-68 landed ______ Edwards Air Force Base.” The blank connects the verb landed to its location. A preposition must link a verb to a noun phrase directly, with no intervening punctuation. Any mark between a preposition and its object violates Standard English conventions.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "at—": An em dash after the preposition creates an abrupt break between at and Edwards Air Force Base. A preposition must connect directly to its object; inserting a dash severs that connection. ✗
  • "at,": A comma after the preposition separates at from its noun phrase. Prepositions are not followed by commas before their objects in Standard English. ✗
  • "at:": A colon introduces what follows only when a complete independent clause precedes it. “Space shuttle flight STS-68 landed at” is not a complete independent clause, so the colon is grammatically unjustified. ✗
  • "at": The bare preposition at directly precedes its object Edwards Air Force Base with no intervening punctuation, which is the only correct way to join a preposition to its noun phrase. ✓
That matches the correct answer — D is correct.
